sentences.text: After the-- this SS animal couldn't find nobody over there, because nobody say, I am from the Sonderkommando.
But many people, they wanted to go o-- to get some dead bodies from around and bury them, because they had a trench over there they were burying the people were dying over there.
They wanted to go out.
Maybe I was thinking to go out too, to go and get that kind of-- from the dead body, kind of a gold tooth or something, just to give to somebody for-- because the hungry was the worst of everything.
When you hungry, you worse than an animal.
Hungry is the worst can happen to-- to a human being.
But I said, [LAUGHS] no.
It was so cold.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after that-- that gentleman went out, then I sat down the way I was before.
So the next day they came around.
And he said-- they said-- I didn't see this guy anymore.
They said, who wants to go to work out to my house and somewhere, because B-52 were coming and bumping all over.
There was big holes they were doing.
Those Germans, they were scared from these B-52.
They were shaking all over when they heard the B-52s.
So my brother went first.
I don't know, Dario maybe too, because I was all alone after that.
So my brother said, I'll go.
And he came to me and said, brother, you're going to leave?
All right.
Go leave.
I'll go.
Maybe one of us two is going to live.
And this way-- this way in Mauthausen.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, I got beat over there.
Somebody was almost dying and so on.
Well, I was sitting somewhere [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] near to the-- those wires, the electric wires.
Over there was some trash.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that stuff was smelling too bad, because of the potatoes-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
--skins.
Was peeling the potatoes.
The skins were put in there.
And within two, three days, they smelled so bad.
But I didn't care.
I-- I went over there.
And the post was not too far away from me.
I was going in-- some-- somehow to go to the trash over there to get some of these potato skin, stinking potato skins.
Over there, some guy was working over there.
Was German, I don't know?
No soldier.
One of us.
And he came into near to me and he got the shovel, a heavy shovel, and he hit me.
He wanted to-- to hit me on the head.
I put my hand.
I reached.
I said, no, no.
I was screaming like this.
And he hit me like over here and on the sides.
So right away I couldn't breathe anymore, because he broke some ribs.
He broke some ribs.
And over here was coming a vein out, so on.
And I went running my tent, those tents over there.
I went run over there.
And I told my friends, I'm dying, so goodbye to everybody and so on.
But I lived.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] About two, three, four days after that, they took us from Mauthausen and we start walking, walking and walking and walking and to walking and walking.
Like I told you, Dario knows the name of this-- of this camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Was a provisionary camp.
They made it just like that from the trees.
Over there, they died-- so many people they die [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and-- because was full of lice, contaminated water from typhus.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And over there, died the second brother of this Raul I told you, the German shot him.
He died from tuberculosis.
He was near to me.
He die over there.
I didn't care about tuberculosis or nothing.
He died right in front of me.
And from there-- from there it was one week, two weeks, I don't remember exactly.
So somebody brought us some packages from the Red Cross, chocolate in it, a few canned goods.
So one package for three, four person, five, six person, you know.
And we got some real food from this package from the Red Cross.
And we woke up.
We hear that Hitler dies.
He killed himself.
And over there was not wires around, were only the guards from that Wehrmacht, not SS, because if they were an SS, maybe they were going to shoot us.
Was the Wehrmacht.
And we see them-- we didn't see them anymore.
When we didn't see them anymore, with a couple of friends of mine we start walking, going the way the Americans were coming.
So going, going, going, going, going, going like that.
We couldn't walk anymore.
We didn't see no Americans.
And we stopped in a little village.
There was a barn.
In the barn, we went up on top of the upstairs of the barn.
Over there were some chickens.
So I'm laying down.
I couldn't walk anymore.
And I was burning a fever.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I told my friend, go ahead and get one chicken.
Make-- boil it to have a chicken soup, something.
Well, he didn't get it.
He was laying the egg, the chicken.
When he was laying the eggs, I told him, you can get it now.
She went-- he went to get the chicken.
And he brought it to me.
He said, I cannot kill him, the chicken.
Give it to me, I said.
I got the chicken head, you know.
I was Sonderkommando.
That was a chicken for me.
I killed the chicken, you know.
But we didn't eat nothing I remember.
Maybe we ate some raw chicken like this.
And then the next day, the next morning, we start walking again.
And we found the American Army.
When we found the American Army, the happiest day of our lives.
They were throwing us chocolates, cigarettes, canned foods.
And many people that we got liberated like this way, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can tell you that 50% dies, because we were eating and eating and eating, never get food.
Eating raw meat.
They found raw meat in the villages.
Eating-- whatever we can find, they were eating and eating and eating, until the American guys they got us and they spray us with DDT, because we were full of lice and so on.
And after that, I opened mine eyes in an hospital, three months later.
I was for three months in an hospital in coma.
I guess the American Army, they took me to the hospital because I fainted, I guess.
And I opened the eyes in the hospital in Austria, I guess, I don't know, something like that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] After I got-- I opened mine eyes over there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I couldn't walk.
I couldn't get up.
And I was dirty all the time, because I had abdominal typhus.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I dirty all the bed all the time.
I tried to get up to go to the bathroom and I was dirty all over.
And the German over there, German nurses, [INAUDIBLE], they were getting me, cleaning me up and so on.
But when I opened the eyes, I saw over there an American doctor, officer, and a German doctor, both together.
So this is the way I got liberated, three months after.
And they took me-- after that, when I got stronger, after convalescence home, the German nurses was giving me a bath and food.
I got a shower and so on.
When I was a little stronger, they sent me to Italy, Bari.
From Bari, I went to Athens.
In Athens, I found Dario and we went to stay in the house of his aunt.
What-- what can I say, Carol?
The only thing, I wanted to get-- to feel good, to feel stronger.
As long as they were giving me the soup or the food that I needed, and always was coming doctor German and American, not only the German.
We knew that they killed them.
I didn't know where Dario was or where my brother was.
After that, in Athens, I learned from Dario, because Dario was with my brother, the one who's in Italy now, I got his picture, the one showing the number.
He was strong-- strong like a bull.
He's two, three years younger than I am.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When he got liberated, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with the trucks they took them to Italy.
And then in Italy, all-- all of them, they were checking by doctors.
So when the turn of my brother came, the doctor checked him.
He said, you stay [NON-ENGLISH], back to the side.
After when they go to him again, they got some X-rays and so on.
And my brother start to spit blood from his mouth.
This I learned from Dario.
And then I got in touch with my brother in Italy.
I don't know how.
I don't remember how I got in touch with my brother.
And that poor guy, he was in the hospital for 10 years, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and still suffering, of course.
Like I'm suffering, I got all these voices from the crematories, like coming from the catacombs underground, [MOANING].
And I was running and running, running.
I still got them in mine-- mine ears.
If I was what pick up?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Well, we were free over there.
Yeah.
We were going over there to sleep.
And I don't remember from whom we got help, from the community over there, Jewish community, or maybe it was the America Joint Distribution Committee.
Was very difficult, the traveling at that time.
I was in Bari maybe week, two weeks.
Somebody else was more than that.
It was at the liberation.
Who knows, because I woke up three months later.
I don't know.
Ask Dario.
[LAUGHS] He was waiting for me.
My sister.
The older one.
Of course, the other two died.
Rachel.
Oh.
In Athens, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] over there for-- again from the American Family Service, they rent a very big house.
It was a double house over there.
And Greek, they were staying in them, that was [NON-ENGLISH].
That means was the house for the displaced girls, which they didn't have nobody.
And they-- over there they were sleeping over there, eating over there.
Some of them going to the Athens [NON-ENGLISH], to go to Israel, like my sister.
I found my sister in there.
Then she went to the [NON-ENGLISH].
The [NON-ENGLISH], they went by boat to Israel, which the boat was caught by the English.
And I don't know how long they were in the boat before they let them go again and so on.
The what?
Yeah.
Together.
Together.
As matter of fact, my-- my sister lived is because of her fiance, because her fiance was a technician in doing the barracks and was going around here and there, here and there.
And he could get a piece of portion more than what we had.
And then he was helping my sister for food, something like that.
This is the way she lived.
No right after.
It took a little while, maybe a couple of months I guess.
Nothing.
My house destroyed from some refugees, no windows, no doors, no [INAUDIBLE].
They burn everything for wintertime, to get some heat.
And then all mine friends, all my friends-- from Salonika, we were 65,000 in Greece.
The only thing we return over there was 1 and 1/2 percent, so the other guys, they didn't have no home, no nothing.
They start to get married and so on.
Some people in [NON-ENGLISH] too.
They put them in the [NON-ENGLISH], which from the community that they had some temples or synagogues or some homes like that for poor people.
They were sleeping over there.
Oh.

In Salonika, the only thing I went over there is to how I got this picture of my mother and my father, because over there-- I was over there and I found the old neighbors, which they saved those pictures.
That was the only way.
Oh, not too long, maybe a week, because I was married at the time and I had a little girl in Athens.
That's why when I went to Salonika-- was the second time, yeah.
The second time I stood maybe a week, two weeks.
Right.
Right.
I was-- I was working at American Joint Distribution Committee at the time with Dario.
What I was doing?

I was working in a warehouse of the Jewish-- of the American Joint, going to the port with the trucks, getting all the clothes and many other things they were sending us from-- from over here and then take them to the warehouse, and then the warehouse, putting everything together, soap, medicine, clothes shoes, and so on.
And there was some other guy who was taking care of the families.
What do you need, this?
What do you need?
You were given a receipt, go ahead and get it from the warehouse.
This what I was doing over there.
How did you meet your wife?
Oh.
I met my wife in a photograph studio.
Was a little room of photograph studio, because when we went in Athens, I rent that studio on top of the building.
We were working over there with this Isaac Venezia I told you, which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he lived from the crematoria when it was burning and he went to the Kanada-Kommando.
That guy was a technician to make briefcases, suitcases of leather.
So we want to make a living over there, so I rent that place and we're making this kind of business, until they get us.
But this place was rented still in my name.
We would go-- we went back with Dario over there.

Over there I had a big container [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with some stuff in it, some clothes, some-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] many things from home, from my other two brothers, [NON-ENGLISH]-- Italian citizen, those two, which they got killed.
But I still had that container over there.
I went to Dario to see what is in there.
So we found those things and we-- one suit for me, one suit for him, whatever.
But the place I rented to another friend of mine, a photograph.
Most of the photographs I got from him over there.
As a photograph, I rented to him.
My wife was working to the next door when a guy was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a suit-maker-- what is that?
A tailor.
A tailor.
Was working a tailor.
So I met my wife over there.
Her name was Nina.
I don't remember.
Daisy.
She was born in 1949.
1951, because the American Jewish Family Service was closed already [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a year before.
And I was working for the community of Athens as a driver, a Jeep driver, taking the doctors here and there, convalesence homes for the people who were suffering, sicks, and so on.
This was my job in Athens.
No.
Oh, with friends, friends, sure.
It's unbelievable.
Nobody can believe it.
Carol, it's not human beings in this world that can believe it for what happened, except the people who was working over there.
Oh, for example, the few-- we are 5 or 10 of us still living from the Sonderkommando that we saw what was going over there.
And I forgot to mention it.
One day, the people were laid down on the corridor in the-- the oven was in front of it.
They were-- they were passing by those guys taking off the teeth, the gold teeth.
And one time, we heard somebody singing.
[SINGING] There where he was dead, he got up just like that, not on his feet, but sitting down.
He got up and he was singing.
He didn't die at the time.
Was not-- was not on his sense, I mean.
He didn't know what's going-- what was going on.
And soon as the Germans saw him, we went over there, bump.
That was-- that was one thing.
And another thing I forgot to mention it, the only time that-- that happened, right in the gas chambers, after we opened the doors, we heard some baby cries.
Was on top of the older dead people was a baby over there crying.
And the German, when he saw it-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Can you believe that, Carol?
No books.
No pictures.
Like I told you before, it's impossible to imagine.
They got an idea, of course, they were [INAUDIBLE], and so on.
But the suffering they got over there, all those things.
Everybody of us has got his own story.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I can say again, God bless America.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If it wasn't for the America Army, many thousand more they were going to get killed, many, many, many.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were wonderful to us.
The most important things I told you, Carol, the most-- most important.
No.
No.
The only thing is the two guys who hided me under them on top of me-- so in 1971, for the first time after 20 years living over here in this country, I went to Israel with my wife, Athena.
And we went over there I think to a bar mitzvah or to-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what was it?
Anniversary or a big party, because in Israel they do big parties, beautiful.
I went with my sister, my brother-in-law, [INAUDIBLE].
So I start walking to go in the-- the room over there, the ball room.
At the-- of course, I saw a few people, which they knew me from [INAUDIBLE] years, which most of them I recognize.
Most of them I didn't recognize, but they recognize me.
So I was ready to go into the room.
And two guys standing over there, Venezia, Venezia, calling.
I look at them.
You don't know-- you don't know us?
I said, I don't know.
Maybe somebody from [INAUDIBLE] years.
I don't remember.
Do you remember that night, they said, when that guy, the SS came looking for Sonderkommando and you laid down and we sit down on top of you?
I could believe it, Carol.
How did they recognize me?
Because it was impossible to recognize me or me recognize them, because we were like this.
We were some other people.
We're not the same.
They recognized me and they remind me they were sitting on top of me.
No.
No.
I don't.
I became 75 years old already, Carol.
What-- I don't care anymore and I cannot remember anymore.
Oh.
First they put us to one hotel.
And then from a Jewish family over here, after the hotel they found us an apartment.
Well, I didn't belong to come in Los Angeles.
I was going to Ohio, Cleveland, Ohio.
But in the meantime, Dario came before me.
And he wrote to me, [INAUDIBLE], he said, don't make a big mistake and go to Ohio, Cleveland, because you won't be able to stay over there from the cold and so on and so and so.
So and then I asked them, where should I go?
I said-- do whatever you can and come to Los Angeles, because he went-- he came to Los Angeles.
From Ohio, he came to Los Angeles.
This is what I did.
I was in New York.
My little girl was very sick.
I stay in New York for about 18 days, taking my little girl to the hospitals every day, because during the day she was OK.
At nighttime, she was with fever.
The same thing happened over here until they made so many tests and so on, and she passed away.
So from New York, I told them I cannot go to Cleveland with my little girl so sick and so on.
I'll-- what I'm going to do in Cleveland?
I got friends in Los Angeles and so on.
And they transferred me in Los Angeles.
That's the way I came here.
And what kind of work did you do when you came here?
When I came here, maybe for one month I didn't work.
And after that, from the-- again from the Jewish family, they sent me to work in a kind of a of chairs and tables.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were giving us at that time $1.17 an hour.

And after three months, I guess they were going to give us $1.25 an hour, something like that, because after when we came the three months to get a few cents more an hour, they laid us-- they laid-- they laid off us and they took some other new people.
They never gave us the few cents more an hour.
And after that, I went to work for [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] was a little better.
